reti-tartakower-1910-2d0490.narrative_intro: Vienna, 1910. Richard Reti, soon to be a hypermodern pioneer, has White against Savielly Tartakower. Black has just grabbed a knight on e4, expecting a normal game — but the position is about to detonate. You are White.

17.annotation: Qd8+!! gives up the queen on an empty square - Black's king has to capture, and the engine confirms a forced mate in three. This is the only move that wins on the spot; every quieter try lets the moment pass.

alt_annotations.d1e1: Re1 keeps a small plus and is the engine's pick among the quiet moves, but the moment passes - Black untangles and the game goes on.
alt_annotations.g1f3: Nf3 develops, but Black's queen slips away with tempo and even grabs a pawn on the queenside - the line ends with Black on top.
alt_annotations.d2b4: Bb4 eyes the dark squares, but it hands Black time to develop and shore up the defense - the attack never lands.
alt_annotations.d3e2: Qe2 saves the queen but concedes the initiative; Black develops comfortably and stands clearly better.

19.annotation: Bg5+!! is the point of the queen sacrifice: a check Black can only answer by walking the king out, and the engine shows forced mate next move. The alternatives leave Black firmly in control - this is the only move that justifies what came before.

alt_annotations.g1f3: Nf3 lets the attack stall; Black weathers the storm, gives some material back on his own terms, and comes out clearly on top.
alt_annotations.d2a5: Ba5+ is a check Black meets calmly - the king steps aside and the attack runs out of steam with White far behind.
alt_annotations.d2f4: Bf4+ picks the queen back up after the check, but Black's counterblows land first - the engine has Black clearly winning.
alt_annotations.d2c3: Bc3+ is the same regain-the-queen idea, and it fails the same way: Black keeps the initiative and White ends up lost.

21.annotation: Bd8# - the bishop delivers mate while the rook covers the king's escape squares, and the queen sacrifice is fully repaid. The mate was forced either way: had the king stepped back instead, the rook would have landed the mate on the same square. The quiet tries simply lose: the attack evaporates and Black takes over.

alt_annotations.g5e3: Be3 trades blows and leaves Black clearly winning - the moment for the finish is gone.
alt_annotations.g1f3: Nf3 lets Black force a queen trade and grab the bishop; the endgame is lost for White.
alt_annotations.f2f4: f4 chases the queen but the bishop falls - Black wins comfortably.
alt_annotations.g1h3: Nh3 develops the wrong piece at the wrong time; the bishop falls and the game with it.

morphy-isouard-1858-f7f676.narrative_intro: Paris Opera House, 1858. Paul Morphy, the American prodigy, is at the board with White against two aristocrats sharing the black pieces. They would rather watch the opera — and Morphy is about to make the game very short. You are White.

17.annotation: Bg5 brings the last minor piece into play and keeps Black tied up - the king is still in the centre and development is badly behind. At +2.5 White is clearly better; the slower tries let Black start to untangle.

alt_annotations.c1e3: Be3 is solid but slower - Black hits back on the queenside and trades away some of the pressure.
alt_annotations.a2a4: a4 is a useful clamp but too slow; Black develops and the advantage shrinks.
alt_annotations.e1g1: O-O is safe, but it spends the critical moment - Black gets coordinated and White's edge fades.
alt_annotations.g2g4: g4 is ambitious but loosening; Black counters on the queenside and the advantage dwindles.

19.annotation: Nxb5! grabs a pawn and tears at the queenside just as Black hoped to gain space there. Even Black's best defense - checks and a queen trade - leaves White at +3.3 with the attack rolling.

alt_annotations.g5f6: Bxf6 trades off the defender too early; after the recapture Black gets to consolidate and the advantage shrinks.
alt_annotations.c4b5: Bxb5 wins the pawn the slow way - after the recapture the attack is gone and it's nearly level.
alt_annotations.c4e2: Be2 is too modest; Black completes development and White's initiative disappears.
alt_annotations.c4d5: Bd5 looks flashy but Black just accepts - the trades leave only a tiny edge.

23.annotation: Castling long does two jobs at once: the king reaches safety and the rook enters the game with immediate effect. At +5.5 every white piece is working and Black's defense is stretched to breaking.

alt_annotations.g5f6: Bxf6 is still clearly better for White, but it releases the grip a notch - the precise move order matters here.
alt_annotations.a2a3: a3 is far too slow; Black's queen finds counterplay and most of the advantage is gone.
alt_annotations.e1g1: O-O castles into the wrong plan: Black's queen check turns into a raid that costs White heavily in the line.

25.annotation: Rxd7! rips away the defender. Black must recapture, and the forcing sequence that follows wins material while the attack keeps rolling - the engine reads +5.4 and climbing. The slower moves let Black breathe.

alt_annotations.d1d3: Rd3 keeps a big plus but hands Black a tempo to organize - the win takes far longer.
alt_annotations.b5a4: Ba4 is a fine regrouping idea, but it gives Black a breath; still better for White, much less forcing.
alt_annotations.d1d5: Rd5 invites harassment; Black gains time and hangs on.
alt_annotations.c1b1: Kb1 is a tidy safety move, but the position asks for action - Black gets to reinforce.

27.annotation: Rd1 brings the last piece into the attack with no loss of momentum. At +5.2 Black has no good way to untangle, and every alternative gives the defense real breathing room.

alt_annotations.g5f6: Bxf6 cashes in too soon - the trades ease Black's defense; White stays better but the grip loosens.
alt_annotations.c2c3: c3 is a quiet move when the position demands urgency; Black reorganizes.
alt_annotations.b5a4: Ba4 retreats at the key moment; the attack cools and Black holds together longer.
alt_annotations.f2f4: f4 opens lines Black can use too; the advantage drops sharply.

29.annotation: Bxd7+ forces the issue: Black's recapture is compelled, the king stays exposed, and the forcing line wins material - +7.0 with the finish in sight.

alt_annotations.g5f6: Bxf6 is nearly as strong - both moves win - but the master's choice is the most forcing path.
alt_annotations.b3c3: Qc3 keeps a large advantage but releases the pressure for a moment, and Black gets options.
alt_annotations.d1d7: Rxd7 is the right capture with the wrong piece: Black's queen strikes back at White's and the win turns messy.
alt_annotations.b3e6: Qxe6+ trades queens and wins a piece in the line, but the attack loses its punch - much less than the master's move.

31.annotation: Qb8+!! gives up the queen - the knight is forced to take, and that single deflection decides: the engine confirms forced mate next move. The quieter queen moves keep a big advantage but let the forced finish slip.

alt_annotations.b3b7: Qb7 keeps a winning position, but the defense gets to keep playing - the immediate end is gone.
alt_annotations.b3c3: Qc3 is the same story: still much better for White, but the forced finish slips away.
alt_annotations.b3b5: Qb5 walks into Black's regrouping; nearly all of the advantage evaporates.
alt_annotations.d1d5: Rd5 loses the thread entirely - Black consolidates and the game is level again.

33.annotation: Rd8# - the rook delivers mate while the bishop covers the king's escape squares, completing the queen sacrifice. Anything else throws the game away: Black's counterattack takes over.

alt_annotations.f2f4: f4 declines the finish and loses outright - Black's queen raids and the position collapses.
alt_annotations.g5h4: Bh4 retreats into a lost position; Black takes over completely.
alt_annotations.c1b1: Kb1 passes - Black mobilizes and wins.
alt_annotations.g5e3: Be3 lets Black's queen snap off a pawn, and the attack flips the other way.
